coredata.dc:title: Experimental, electrochemical and DFT simulation studies of a novel Schiff base derivative as an efficient mild steel corrosion inhibitor in acidic environments
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: K, Shashirekha
Aithal, Shubhrajyotsna
B.M, Praveen
M.K, Pavithra
Guruprasad, A.M.
Devendra, Bharath K.
Rathod, Manohar R.
coredata.dc:description: A newly synthesized Schiff base, consisting 3-methyl-2-{(E)-[(2E)-3-phenylprop-2-en-1-ylidene] amino} butanoic acid, is utilized to prevent mild steel from corroding in acidic environments. FTIR spectra validate the prepared SB. The corrosion rate at various temperatures was measured using electrochemical techniques like as polarization and electrochemical impedance tests. At a higher temperature of 333K, this molecule exhibits about 90% inhibition efficacy with 50 ppm inhibitor. The corrosion inhibition mechanism was measured using thermodynamic and adsorption characteristics. These findings suggest that chemical adsorption causes a surface layer to form on the metal surface. The Langmuir adsorption isotherm is followed. The creation of the adsorption layer on the metal surface is confirmed by SEM and AFM pictures. Quantum chemical calculations were calculated and discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Corrosion
Schiff’s base
Mild steel
AFM
SEM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectrum obtained for SB molecule.
Tafel plots at (A) 303 K, (B) 313 K, (C) 323 K and (D) 333 K.
OCP graph for MS in 1 M HCl using SB inhibitor.
AC Impedance plots for MS at (A) 303 K, (B) 313 K, (C) 323 K and (D) 333 K.
Equivalent circuit model.
Curve fitting diagram for 50 ppm at 303 K.
Langmuir adsorption isotherm plots.
A plot of lnKads vs 1/T.
Arrhenius plot of MS without and with SB in 1 M HCl.
Transition state plots of MS without and with SB in 1M HCl.
(a) HOMO (b) LUMO (c) Electrostatic Potential (d) Contours map for molecules.
Final adsorption of studied SB molecules on the iron (110) surface achieved by MD simulations.
(a) and 13 (b): The study examined the surface morphology of mild steel both with and without inhibitors.
AFM images for surface roughness.
Tafel results in a temperature region of 303 K–333 K. The following points can be noted from the Tafel data.
AC Impedance results in a temperature region of 303 K–333 K.
Thermodynamics parameters of absorption of SB on MS in 1M HCl.
Activation parameters of MS in 1M HCl containing different concentrations of SB.
Stability of the molecules by EHOMO and ELUMO.
MD parameters for studied SB molecule adsorbed on the iron (110) surface achieved by MD simulations.

head.ce:title: Experimental, electrochemical and DFT simulation studies of a novel Schiff base derivative as an efficient mild steel corrosion inhibitor in acidic environments

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A newly synthesized Schiff base, consisting 3-methyl-2-{(E)-[(2E)-3-phenylprop-2-en-1-ylidene] amino} butanoic acid, is utilized to prevent mild steel from corroding in acidic environments. FTIR spectra validate the prepared SB. The corrosion rate at various temperatures was measured using electrochemical techniques like as polarization and electrochemical impedance tests. At a higher temperature of 333K, this molecule exhibits about 90% inhibition efficacy with 50 ppm inhibitor. The corrosion inhibition mechanism was measured using thermodynamic and adsorption characteristics. These findings suggest that chemical adsorption causes a surface layer to form on the metal surface. The Langmuir adsorption isotherm is followed. The creation of the adsorption layer on the metal surface is confirmed by SEM and AFM pictures. Quantum chemical calculations were calculated and discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel is the most common material used in various industries and building materials because of its low cost and good mechanical strength (Abiola et al., 2007; Haque et al., 2021; Hebbar et al., 2015). Acid solutions are used for these applications such as pickling, descaling, etching and cleaning. Corrosion is a main problem associated with this mechanism (Idora et al., 2015; Zarrok et al., 2023; Devendra et al., 2022; Ali et al., 2024; Pais and Rao, 2023). In this process, inhibitors are used to control the corrosion. Inhibitors are organic/Inorganic compounds and these are adhered on the metals surface to control the corrosion.

It is well known that organic compounds containing heteroatoms (N, O, S, etc.) inside aromatic moieties operate as effective corrosion inhibitors in a variety of corrosive conditions (Saraswat et al., 2024; Mehta et al., 2022). Lone pair electrons of these compounds are making the bond with the metals and creates the passive layer on the metal surface by it controls the corrosion (Chauhan et al., 2021; Alahiane et al., 2023; Praveen et al., 2021; Melian et al., 2023). This homogeneous layer lessens the amount of corrosion by isolating the metal surface from the hostile medium. Because Schiff’s bases include the –CN– group, they are also employed as effective corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in HCl conditions (Desai et al., 2024; Varadaraj et al., 2022). The molecule’s planarity and the lone pair of electrons on the N and S atoms aid in the molecule’s adsorption to the metal surface (Shylesha et al., 2012). Given their non-toxic and biodegradable nature, it is important to investigate their anticorrosive properties, particularly in light of the current emphasis on producing inhibitors with minimal environmental impact (Shylesha et al., 2011).

As well known, some authors have explained that in addition to the pi electrons from the Schiff bases entering the transition metals' unoccupied orbitals, the * orbital can also accept the electrons from the d-orbitals of the same metals to form feedback bonds (Popoola et al., 2019), which allows the metal-inhibitor bond to exist. Additionally, Schiff bases have recently been found to be efficient copper, aluminium, and steel corrosion inhibitors in acidic conditions. The following are the main benefits of Schiff bases (Prabhu et al., 2006; Singh and Quraishi, 2010; Aourabi et al., 2021; Ahmed et al., 2018). (i) They can be easily synthesized from cheap raw materials. (ii) They have an electron cloud on the aromatic ring or, in relatively long chain compounds, electronegative elements like nitrogen and sulphur. (iii) Harmless for environment, which encouraged us to test this type of inhibitor (Jayaperumal, 2010).

In the present study, Schiff base is prepared by cinnamaldehyde and L-valine. It is used to study the effect on mild steel corrosion inhibition. Both theoretical and experimental studies were used to study the corrosion inhibition studies.
Schiff base of Cinnamaldehyde and L-valine was prepared by the standard procedure. All the chemicals were used LR grade. Prepared compound was characterised by TLC and FTIR spectra.

The electrochemical workstation CHI 608D was utilized for conducting electrochemical studies. A platinum counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a steel working electrode made up the setup. The working electrode was submerged in the test solution for 30 min before to each measurement in order to establish a steady-state open-circuit potential (OCP). Every potential that was obtained was compared to the Ag/AgCl electrode (Matad et al., 2014).

The electrochemical parameters such as corrosion potential (Ecorr), corrosion current density (icorr) and inhibition efficiency according to polarization studies (ɳ
p ) are listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impedance data were fitted to most appropriate equivalent circuit by using ZSimp Win 3.21 software
ce:section.ce:para: The impedance parameters were obtained from Nyquist plot are listed inTable 2 . Sum of charge transfer resistance (Rct) and adsorption resistance (Rad) is equivalent to polarization resistance (Rp).
In order to know the adsorption of the inhibitor, different adsorption was tried at different temperature and based on the fit thermodynamic and activation parameters were calculated.

The density functional theory (DFT) approach was utilized to ascertain the quantum characteristics of the inhibitor that was under investigation. The inhibitor’s geometrical structure was optimized using Becke’s three-parameter exchange functional B3LYP non-local correlation functional, which used 6-311 + G (d, p) as the fundamental set. The molecules were designed using Gauss View, version 6.0, performed in the Gaussian 09 software package (Bentiss et al., 2009).

Studies on corrosion protection have increasingly relied on molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Generally, MD simulations are used to better understand an inhibitor molecule’s interactions and adsorption on the substrate surface. Our system, which included 500 H2O, 5 H3O+, 5 Cl- and medication molecule, was successfully developed in the current work. The Materials Studio software program using Forcite code was used to run MD simulations in a simulation box with dimensions of 24.85 × 24.85 × 40.71 Å3, in a unit cell of 8 × 8, and a vacuum of 20 Å. The MD simulations were performed at 298.0 K in the NVT canonical ensemble with an Anderson thermostat, using the COMPASS force field and periodic boundary conditions.
SEM and AFM are measured in a 1 M HCl solution both with and without an inhibitor.

Schiff base was characterised by FTIR spectra, Fig. 1 . Alkenyl CC along with Carbonyl CO observed in the range 1600 to 1650 cm−1. The formation of Schiff’s base confirmed by the presence of characteristic high intensity imine linkage –CN in the range 1500-1600 cm−1. Broad stretching band near 3450 cm−1 confirming the presence of (OH−) hydroxyl group. The absorption band due to aromatic hydrogen appeared in the region 1400-1500 cm-1.

When the concentration and the number of inhibitor molecules adsorbed on the metal surface rise, the rate of corrosion gradually decreases. From Fig. 2 , it is observed that, when compared to the uninhibited solution, the inhibited solution’s corrosion potential (Ecorr) value slightly changed. If Ecorr is less than ±85 mv, the inhibitor is a mixed-type inhibitor. Currently available research shows that the present inhibitor operates as a mixed type inhibitor for the corrosion of mild steel in acidic medium, with a maximum displacement of Ecorr of around 30 mv. Therefore, the observed value indicates that larger concentrations have better inhibitory efficiencies. It is concluded that there aren’t any more adsorbed molecules on the metal surface, and these compounds exhibit inhibition (de Oliveira Ramos et al., 2012; Musa et al., 2010). The given inhibitor shows more than 90 % inhibition efficiency and also it shows good inhibitor at higher temperature. It is a very effective inhibition values observed in the present work. SB molecule in corrosive environment serves as an adsorption inhibitor, slowing the corrosion process by blocking active sites. There is no substantial variation in cathodic Tafel slope (βa) and anodic Tafel slope (βc) values, indicating that it does not change the corrosion reaction mechanism (Matad et al., 2014b).

In order to study the effect of stability of the inhibitor, we have kept the inhibitor for 96 h in corrosive medium. Weight loss occurred by corrosion of the metal is same as 2 h and 96 h. It shows that our studied compound is stable for long duration for 100 h. The change in OCP (Open Circuit Potential) with immersion time for MS in the presence and absence of SB inhibitor in 1 M HCl is depicted in Fig. 3 .

Electrochemical impedance results confirm the Tafel results. Here also it shows around 90 percentage inhibition efficiency and it retains at higher temperature too. The formed passive layer of inhibitor on the metal surface acts as barrier for the metal and environment and it prevents the corrosion of metal. Polarization resistance of the metal increases with inhibitor concentration and also diameter of semi-circle increases (Fig. 4 .) with the concentration of the inhibitor. Double layer capacitance values are also decreases with inhibitor concentration (Soror and El-Ziady, 2003) (see Fig. 5

Electrochemical studies taken by EIS and Polarization results are similar in nature. Inhibitor molecule forms the passive layer on the metal surface. It reduces the exposure of metal to the corrosive environment and it reduces the corrosion. It is proven by polarization resistance values and double layer capacitance values. Polarization resistance values increases with concentration and double layer capacitance values decreases with concentration of the inhibitor. Tafel slopes values also almost similar in nature. Corrosion current decreases with inhibitor concentration and tends to constant values after optimum value. This shows the saturation of adsorption of the inhibitor. Corrosion rate is more in higher temperature and it is proved in both EIS and polarization studies. The data obtained from EIS measurement are fit to an equivalent circuit as shown in Fig. 5. The experimental curve fit precisely with the curve obtained by the electrical equivalent circuit, in Fig. 6 .

Interaction of SB with MS surface, the obtained polarization data were fitted with various absorption isotherms. Th best fit was obtained for Langmuir absorption as shown in Fig. 7 . Langmuir adsorption isotherm is represented by equation.
(1)
C / θ = 1 /
K ads
+ C
where C represents the SB concentration, Kads is the adsorption equilibrium constant, and θ is the degree of surface covering indicated by % IE/100 (Mehta et al., 2022).

Table 3 shows the Kads values that were derived from the straight-line intercept of Fig. 8 . Moreover, the equation relates Kads to the standard free energy of adsorption ΔG*ads.
(2)
K
a d s
= 1 / 55.5
exp
(
‐ Δ
G
a d s
0
/ R T
)

Table 3 presents the ΔG0 ads values that were calculated. The chart makes it clear that the strong adsorption behaviour of SB molecules on the MS surface is what causes the larger values of Kads. Further, ΔG0 ads are negative and which indicate spontaneous adsorption of SB. Moreover, in the present study, ΔG0 ads values obtained are in between −20 kJmol-1 and -40 kJmol-1 which supports lie comprehensive adsorption mechanism. During adsorption, SB molecules undergo physisorption followed by chemisorption on MS surface. Also, as the T increase ΔG0 ads also increases which reveals the exothermic in nature of adsorption process.

On the other hand, the standard enthalpy of adsorption ΔH*ads and standard entropy of adsorption ΔS0 ads are related to Kads by Van’t Hoff equation.
(3)
ln
K ads
= ‐
Δ
H ads 0
/ RT + Δ
S ads 0
/ R + ln 1 / 55.5

And they are calculated from the intercept and slope of ln Kads vs 1/Tplot. The calculated ΔH0 ads and ΔS0 ads values are 110.7 kJmol-1 and 454.5 kJmol-1 respectively. The negative value of ΔH0 ads reflects the exothermic process of adsorption of SB on MS. Also, ΔS0 ads is negative since the exothermic adsorption is associated with the decrease of entropy.

In general, corrosion processes are considered as Arrhenius processes. The activation parameters can be obtained from equation (Idora et al., 2015) and (Zarrok et al., 2023) which are given below for Arrhenius and transition state equation respectively.
(4)
InI corr
= InA ‐
Ea RT
(5)
InI corr
T
=
[
In R / Nh +
Δ S
*
R
]
‐
Δ H
*
RT
where A is Arrhenius pre-exponential factor, Ea* is activation energy, ΔH*mis change in enthalpy and ΔS* is change in entropy of activation.

Ea* values can be calculated from the slope of straight lines of Arrhenius plots given in Fig. 9 . The values of ΔH* and ΔS* are obtained from the slope & intercept of straight lines in transition state plots given shown in Fig. 10 . The activation parameters obtained are given in Table 4 .

Table 4 clearly indicate that Ea* increases with increasing concentration of SB and also & Ea* in inhibited solution is more than uninhibited solution at higher concentration of SB which may be due to increase in the thickness of double layer during corrosion inhibition.

The complex mechanisms and interplay between the chemical interactions of SB molecular orbitals and iron atomic orbitals have been thoroughly studied using quantum chemical approaches (Gupta et al., 2023; Minagalavar et al., 2023; Rathod et al., 2021; Minagalavar et al., 2024). illustrate the wide range of research applications for these methodologies. Density functional theory (DFT) at the Gaussian level is used to depict the ideal geometrical configurations, ground state molecular orbital energies (HOMO, LUMO), and boundary molecular orbitals of the studied active compound in Fig. 11(a) and (b). Furthermore, the charge dispersion location of the active component from the SB inhibitor is highlighted in Fig. 11(c), which sheds insights on the molecule’s total electrostatic influence [31]. The DFT method revealed the electronic characteristics (EHOMO, ELUMO, and Dipole moment). Other quantum chemical parameters, such as the energy gap (ΔEL-H), chemical softness (S), chemical hardness (η), absolute electronegativity (χ), electrophilicity index (ω) and electron transfer from the extracted molecule to the metal surface (ΔN), were assessed utilizing the mathematical equations (6)–(11).
(6)
Δ E =
E
L U M O
−
E
H O M O
(7)
χ =
(
I P + E A
2
)
(8)
η =
(
I P − E A
2
)
(9)
S =
1
2 η
(10)
ω =
μ 2
2 η
(11)
Δ N =
X
m e t a l − X
i n h
2
(
η
m e t a l +
η
i n h
)

An increased electron-donating potential of a molecule to the open ‘d’ orbitals of mild steel Fe atoms is indicated by a larger EHOMO value, which designates EHOMO as an electron donor. The HOMO of the inhibitors may interact with the unoccupied 3d orbitals. Additionally, since the ELUMO frequently indicates a molecule’s propensity to receive electrons, a filled 4s orbital might contribute electrons to the LUMO of the SB inhibitor. As a result, as ELUMO values decrease, the possibility of the molecules obtaining electrons increases (Madkour et al., 2018). The results shown in Table 5 indicate that molecules show strong stability since there is a significant energy difference between EHOMO and ELUMO.

Additionally, the contours map in Fig. 11 (d) and electrostatic potential map indicates the overall electrostatic influence caused by the molecules formed at the site during charge dispersion of the active component from the SB inhibitor. This section has involved a study of the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) in order to anticipate physicochemical features, in particular reactive sites for electrophilic and nucleophilic attack as well as places with electrostatic potential. The MEP is a plot of electrostatic potential projected using a 3D visual technique to understand the electrostatic impact of a molecule. Understanding how electron density affects the MEP is essential to understanding how nucleophilic and electrophilic sites react as well as how hydrogen bonds with other molecules. Different colors correspond to different degrees of electrostatic potential at the surface. Red indicates an electron-rich (negative) region, and Fig. 11 (c) illustrates that a significant amount of negative charge is concentrated on the nitrogen and oxygen atoms. Because of this, it is thought that the N and O atoms have the greatest capacity to donate electrons to the production of metal ions or H-bonds. An electron-poor (positive) region by blue colour Fig. 11 (c), and the region with zero electrostatic potential is represented by green color. The positive area (blue colour) of the MEP surfaces is the most suitable position for a nucleophilic attack, whereas the negative area (red colour) is the optimum location for an electrophilic attack.

Additionally, a smaller energy gap makes it easier for the molecule to polarize (Gece and Bilgic, 2009). Since the electronegativity value (χ) measures a molecule’s ability to attract electrons, a higher value denotes a greater ability to obtain electrons (Kavirajwar et al., 2022; Ech-chihbi et al., 2019). The molecule’s good charge transfer resistance increases with its chemical hardness (η) value. If ΔN is smaller than 3.6, the SB is expected to donate one electron to the mild steel surface, according to reports in the literature (Huang and Bu, 2020). It is demonstrated that the inhibitor in this experiment interacts considerably with the metal surface because ΔN (0.5412) is less than 3.6.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation is a well-established, widely utilized theoretical approach in anticorrosion research. Therefore, researchers have focused on developing complex molecular models using MD simulations in this scenario. These provide additional details on interaction mechanisms, inhibitor-preferred orientation, and other factors, such as the expected interaction energy, when the molecules under investigation interact with mild steel. The adsorption of SB molecule on the Fe surface, is described in the current work using MD simulations that have been modified for investigation. The examined molecule provide helpful information regarding how they interact with the iron surface and are also utilized to strengthen and confirm the experiment’s findings. The SB inhibitor molecule was put with the solvent layer containing H2O, H3O+, and Cl− in the MD simulations. The interaction system was created when the energy and temperature variations achieve equilibrium state approaches novel Schiff base derivative molecule adsorption and interaction energy. Fig. 12 , shows the SB inhibitor final adsorption pattern on the surface of Fe (110) in the aqueous medium that show that the studied molecule affirms a nearly flat orientation onto the surface of Fe, which results in an excellent coating of the metal to be protected (Minagalwar et al., 2024). However, the strong interactions between the molecules under examination and the iron atoms are due to the presence of active centres in the aromatic rings and the N and O atom, according to MD simulations of the compound.
(12)
E
Interaction
=
E total
−
(
E
surface + solution
+
E BOLE
(13)
E
B i n d i n g
=
− E
I n t e r a c t i o n

The binding energy of the molecule’s adsorption on Fe (110) is determined after interaction between the components of the solution and the investigated molecule. It results in a value of 172.842 kcal mol−1, demonstrating the molecule’s ability to adsorb on the soft steel surface as a significant positive number in Table 6 . By comparing the binding energy of the investigated molecule’s adsorption with that of the explored for various other Schiff base derivative molecules evaluated by other researchers, it can be seen that this value is substantially higher, which has the effect of causing all the studied Schiff base derivative molecules to form a protective layer on the Fe surface (110) (Pai et al., 2024). As a result, the good binding energy value suggests that each tested molecule seems to have more than one binding iron surface. The MD calculations show that SB molecule have a significant interaction energy value estimated at −172.842 kcal mol−1, which may be the reason for its high experimental performance because of the stable inhibitor/Fe (110) surface interactions. The simulations have shown that the inhibitor SB molecules can efficiently adsorb on the surface of the soft steel to protect it against corrosion (Sarkar et al., 2022).

In Fig. 13 (a) mild steel surface is strongly damaged in acidic medium due to the dissolution of metal. The surface became highly porous. However, it is prevented by dipping in inhibitor solution for 4 h. The surface roughness is reduced and smooth surfaces are formed in Fig. 13 (b). As a result, it acts as protective film on the metal surface which is prevented by the corrosion (Sarkar et al., 2022).

As shown in Fig. 14 , AFM was used to investigate the surface morphology and roughness of mild steel while a corrosion inhibitor was present. This technique looks into the barrier layer that forms on the metal surface and works at the nanoscale level to stop corrosion (Rathod and Rajappa, 2022).

SB has been found to be a highly effective corrosion inhibitor on mild steel, with an inhibition efficacy of about 90% at 50 ppm. This efficiency doesn’t change across a large temperature range. It confirms the formation of adsorption by chemically. Adsorption follows Langmuir method. For studying surface adsorption, DFT simulations are used in conjunction with classical MD simulations. The inhibitor’s method of action on mild steel validates its adsorption. SEM and AFM studies strengthens the layer formed on the metal surface.

Shashirekha K: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Conceptualization. Shubhrajyotsna Aithal: Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis. Praveen B.M: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Pavithra M.K: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Data curation. A.M. Guruprasad: Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Bharath K. Devendra: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Manohar R. Rathod: Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
